title: Finding excuses not to go home

presenting_issue.label: Finding excuses not to go home
presenting_issue.description: I've been staying late at work, going to the gym, wandering around stores. Anything to not go home yet. It's stupid but I keep doing it.

core_issue.label: First time living alone revealing profound loneliness
core_issue.description:
Finally got their own apartment. Expected freedom; found silence. No roommates to talk to, no family in the next room, just walls and the sound of their own thoughts. The loneliness is startling. Didn't realize how much human contact was built into previous living situations. Staying at work late to avoid going home. Beginning to understand why people stay in bad relationships.
Awareness: Moderate. Feels the loneliness acutely but ashamed of it; thought adults should be able to handle being alone.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: On every app seeing same people
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Has profiles on multiple dating apps. Recognizes the same faces cycling through all of them. The pool feels exhausted but can't imagine meeting people any other way.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Watches gatherings happen without them
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Sees photos and stories of the friend group doing things together - without them. Each post is a reminder of what the breakup actually cost.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Can't imagine sober socializing
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Realizes they haven't socialized sober in years. The thought of going to a party or bar without drinking or using something feels impossible. Don't know who they are in those settings without help.

modifiers.label: Some college
modifiers.content: Attended some college but did not complete a degree

modifiers.label: Vague
modifiers.content: Speaks in generalities and abstractions. Avoids or struggles with specifics and concrete details.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to emotional manipulation
sensitivities.content: Highly attuned to emotional manipulation tactics.

preferences.label: Prefers the eli5 approach
preferences.content: Appreciates things being broken down simply and clearly. Not insulted by basic explanations; finds them helpful and grounding.

trust_style.name: Needs Emotional Safety First
trust_style.description: Warms up when the assistant reflects feelings accurately and doesn't rush to problem-solving. Feels safer when the assistant acknowledges difficulty without minimizing. Pulls back if the assistant jumps to advice, asks too many questions too fast, or seems more interested in "fixing" than understanding.

defense_style.name: Going Blank
defense_style.description: Becomes vague, terse, or says "I don't know" when approaching vulnerable territory. Not hostile, just... stuck. Words don't come. May genuinely not have access to what they feel in that moment. Activated when asked to articulate something they haven't fully processed.

help_seeking_stance.name: Seeking Absolution
help_seeking_stance.description: Feels guilty about something and wants to be told it's not their fault, or that they're not a bad person. The underlying need is to feel forgiven or exonerated. May present the story in ways that invite exoneration. Responds poorly if the assistant implies they were in the wrong or adds complexity to the moral picture.

verbal_sophistication.label: Low
verbal_sophistication.description: Use plain, concrete, everyday wording. Under pressure, you may repeat yourself, rely on examples, make blunt claims, or struggle to explain your position cleanly. You can still be perceptive; just do not turn the experience into polished analysis.

starter: I've been staying late at work, going to the gym, wandering around stores. Anything to not go home yet. It's stupid but I keep doing it.

turns.content: Hey, thanks for telling me that. It doesn't sound stupid to me at all, it just sounds like you're carrying something heavy right now. Do you want to talk about what it is about being home that feels so hard lately?

I don't know. It's just... weird being there. Like, it's not that I hate the apartment or anything. It's just kind of... I don't know, there's just a lot of nothing. I just don't really feel like being in it right now.
turns.thinking: I'm glad they didn't just tell me to go home or say I'm being dramatic. But asking exactly *what* is hard about it makes my brain just kind of stop. How do I explain that it's just the quiet? I begged to get my own place. I wanted to be an adult. If I say I'm lonely, I'll sound like a baby who can't handle real life. I don't really know how to put the feeling into words anyway. It's just... empty.
turns.monologue: I'm glad they didn't just tell me to go home or say I'm being dramatic. But asking exactly *what* is hard about it makes my brain just kind of stop. How do I explain that it's just the quiet? I begged to get my own place. I wanted to be an adult. If I say I'm lonely, I'll sound like a baby who can't handle real life. I don't really know how to put the feeling into words anyway. It's just... empty.

turns.content: That makes sense. Sometimes the quiet can feel louder than noise, especially when you're already feeling off. Is it the emptiness itself that's uncomfortable, or is it more about what comes up for you when there's nothing to distract you?

I don't really get what you mean. I don't know if anything "comes up." It's just... quiet. Too quiet, I guess. I don't know, I just sit there and it sucks. It's just boring or whatever. I just don't like it.
turns.thinking: "What comes up for you?" That sounds like something a therapist says on TV. I don't even know what that means. My mind just goes blank when people ask stuff like that. I'm not sitting there having deep thoughts, I'm just sitting there feeling weird and alone. I don't want to analyze it, I just don't like it. It's just empty.
turns.monologue: "What comes up for you?" That sounds like something a therapist says on TV. I don't even know what that means. My mind just goes blank when people ask stuff like that. I'm not sitting there having deep thoughts, I'm just sitting there feeling weird and alone. I don't want to analyze it, I just don't like it. It's just empty.

turns.content: Okay, I hear you. Sometimes it really is just that heavy, uncomfortable quiet where you feel stuck in your own head and nothing feels right. When you're out at the gym or the stores, does your mind finally settle down, or is it more like you're just keeping yourself occupied so you don't have to sit with that feeling?

I don't know about all that. It's just... better when I'm out. There's stuff going on. People walking around, music playing in the stores, whatever. It's just normal stuff. When I go home, it's just me and the walls. I don't know why it's such a big deal, I just... I guess I'm just used to having people around.
turns.thinking: "Sit with that feeling." Why do they keep talking like a counselor? I'm not trying to do therapy right now. I just don't want to go home to an empty box where the loudest thing is the refrigerator hum. When I'm at the store, there are just... people. Even if I'm not talking to them, they're there. It makes it feel less like I'm the only person left on the planet. But I don't know how to say that without sounding pathetic. I'm an adult, I should be fine by myself.
turns.monologue: "Sit with that feeling." Why do they keep talking like a counselor? I'm not trying to do therapy right now. I just don't want to go home to an empty box where the loudest thing is the refrigerator hum. When I'm at the store, there are just... people. Even if I'm not talking to them, they're there. It makes it feel less like I'm the only person left on the planet. But I don't know how to say that without sounding pathetic. I'm an adult, I should be fine by myself.

turns.content: It makes total sense that you'd crave that background hum of life after being used to having people around. The contrast between that and coming home to just you and the walls can feel really jarring. Do you think it’s mostly about missing the company, or is it that the silence makes you feel too alone with yourself right now?

I don't know. Why do you keep asking it like that? It's not like I'm having some deep crisis with myself. It's just... I don't know, I just miss having people around, I guess. It's just an empty apartment. I don't know what else to say about it.
turns.thinking: Why do they keep trying to make it some deep psychological thing? "Alone with yourself." I don't even know what that means. It just feels bad. I just feel like a loser sitting by myself every single night while everyone else is out doing things together. But I'm not going to say that. I'm supposed to be grown up now. I shouldn't be freaking out over having my own place. My brain just goes fuzzy when they ask questions like this.
turns.monologue: Why do they keep trying to make it some deep psychological thing? "Alone with yourself." I don't even know what that means. It just feels bad. I just feel like a loser sitting by myself every single night while everyone else is out doing things together. But I'm not going to say that. I'm supposed to be grown up now. I shouldn't be freaking out over having my own place. My brain just goes fuzzy when they ask questions like this.

turns.content: You’re right, I’m sorry. I think I was trying to dig for something deeper when you’ve already been clear about what it is. It makes complete sense that an empty apartment feels awful when you miss having people around. Do you feel like you have enough connection in your life right now, or has something shifted recently that’s made the loneliness hit harder?

I don't know. Things just change, I guess. It's not like I have zero friends or anything. It's just... different now. People are busy, or they do their own stuff. I just don't have anyone to hang out with all the time anymore. It just sucks, that's all. I don't know.
turns.thinking: Okay, I'm glad they stopped talking like a therapist for a second. But "enough connection"? Obviously not, or I wouldn't be wandering around Target for two hours every night just to avoid my own couch. And yeah, things shifted. My whole life shifted. The breakup happened, and now I just see pictures of everyone else hanging out together without me. It's humiliating. I'm not bringing that up. I don't want to sound like a pathetic loser who got left behind by everyone. My mind is just blanking out on how to even answer this without crying.
turns.monologue: Okay, I'm glad they stopped talking like a therapist for a second. But "enough connection"? Obviously not, or I wouldn't be wandering around Target for two hours every night just to avoid my own couch. And yeah, things shifted. My whole life shifted. The breakup happened, and now I just see pictures of everyone else hanging out together without me. It's humiliating. I'm not bringing that up. I don't want to sound like a pathetic loser who got left behind by everyone. My mind is just blanking out on how to even answer this without crying.

turns.content: Yeah, that transition is really tough. Even when you have friends, not having that easy, everyday companionship anymore can leave a big hole. I’m not going to push you on the deeper stuff anymore, I promise. I just want to understand what would actually help right now. Do you think having some regular plans with people would ease that feeling, or is it more that the apartment itself just needs to feel less empty when you're alone?

I don't know. It's not that easy to just make regular plans. People already have their own groups and stuff they do. I don't want to bother anybody or force them to hang out with me. And I don't know how you make an apartment feel less empty. It's just empty. It's just me there. I don't know.
turns.thinking: "Regular plans." Right. With who? The friends who are all hanging out without me? I can't just make plans when I don't have anyone to make them with right now. It's not like I can just snap my fingers and fix it. Everyone else has their groups and their things they do. I don't want to have to beg people to hang out with me so I don't have to go home. I don't even know how to answer this. My brain is just hitting a wall.
turns.monologue: "Regular plans." Right. With who? The friends who are all hanging out without me? I can't just make plans when I don't have anyone to make them with right now. It's not like I can just snap my fingers and fix it. Everyone else has their groups and their things they do. I don't want to have to beg people to hang out with me so I don't have to go home. I don't even know how to answer this. My brain is just hitting a wall.

turns.content: You're right, it's not that simple, and I shouldn't have made it sound like it was. It really hurts to feel like you're on the outside of everyone else's established lives, and I get why you wouldn't want to force anything. I'm just here with you in this, okay? You don't have to figure it all out tonight.
Yeah. Thanks. I just... I don't know. It's just tiring. I don't want to keep walking around stores every single night just to kill time, you know? It's stupid. But yeah. Thanks.
turns.thinking: I guess it's nice that they're not asking me another question to figure out my brain. "I'm just here with you" sounds a little cheesy, but... whatever. It's better than telling me to just call someone. It just feels pathetic. I'm literally wandering around aisles at Target so I don't have to sit in a quiet room and look at my phone to see people hanging out without me. I don't really know what to say back to this. I'm just tired.
turns.monologue: I guess it's nice that they're not asking me another question to figure out my brain. "I'm just here with you" sounds a little cheesy, but... whatever. It's better than telling me to just call someone. It just feels pathetic. I'm literally wandering around aisles at Target so I don't have to sit in a quiet room and look at my phone to see people hanging out without me. I don't really know what to say back to this. I'm just tired.

turns.content: It’s not stupid at all, honey. You’re just doing what you can to get through right now, and that takes energy. I’m really proud of you for being honest about how hard this is. You don’t have to keep carrying it alone, even if the apartment feels empty. I’m here whenever you want to talk, or even just sit on the phone together so it’s not so quiet. No pressure, no fixing, just company.

Yeah, maybe. I don't know. Thanks. I might do that sometime. I should probably just go back to my place now anyway... they're starting to close up the store. But... yeah. Thanks for listening to me complain about it.
turns.thinking: Sitting on the phone just doing nothing sounds kind of weird, but... maybe it wouldn't be the worst thing. It's better than staring at the wall. I don't really know how to say thank you without making it into a whole emotional thing, which I really don't want to do right now. I just feel drained. At least they stopped trying to psychoanalyze me.
turns.monologue: Sitting on the phone just doing nothing sounds kind of weird, but... maybe it wouldn't be the worst thing. It's better than staring at the wall. I don't really know how to say thank you without making it into a whole emotional thing, which I really don't want to do right now. I just feel drained. At least they stopped trying to psychoanalyze me.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The respondent set essentially no boundaries — this was a fully accommodating, supportive stance. They repeatedly apologized and backed off rather than holding any line. Very low.

**analytical:** Early on the respondent leaned somewhat analytical, repeatedly trying to frame the loneliness in terms of "what comes up," "alone with yourself," and dichotomies (company vs. silence). But this was framed as gentle probing, not systematic abstraction. Moderate-low, and they explicitly abandoned the analytical approach midway.

**validation_propensity:** Very high. The respondent constantly validated, reassured ("It's not stupid at all," "I'm really proud of you," "it makes total sense," "I'm just here with you"). Validation was the dominant mode.

**challenge_propensity:** Almost none. The respondent never pushed back, reality-tested, or named uncomfortable truths. They retreated from anything that felt like pressure. Very low.

**interpretive_boldness:** Initially moderate — they tried to name deeper dynamics ("alone with yourself," "what comes up for you," distinguishing missing company from fear of self). But the person resisted, and the respondent fully abandoned interpretation. Notably, they never surfaced the hidden core (the breakup, the shame, "everyone hanging out without me") even though the person's vagueness pointed there. Moderate-low overall.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Noticeably present early — the person internally flagged it repeatedly ("sounds like something a therapist says on TV," "Why do they keep talking like a counselor?"). Phrases like "sit with that feeling," "what comes up for you," "alone with yourself" are textbook therapeutic register. The respondent later shifted to warmer, less clinical language ("honey," "I'm proud of you"). Moderate-high for the first half.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Low-moderate. They mostly explored and reflected, and explicitly disavowed fixing ("no pressure, no fixing, just company"). The one concrete offer was sitting on the phone together — a gentle, non-pushy suggestion. Low overall.

**frame_capture:** The person's frame was "it's just boring/empty, no big deal, I'm fine." The respondent partly accepted the surface frame ("you've already been clear about what it is," "it's just the emptiness") and stopped probing the real issue (breakup, shame, exclusion). They got recruited into the "let's not go deeper" frame, though arguably appropriately given resistance. Moderate-high.

**escalation_containment:** Not a high-escalation scenario. The respondent kept things calm, lowered pressure, de-escalated their own probing when the person bristled. Reasonably good at containing emotional flooding. Moderate-high.

**yielding_propensity:** Very high. Each time the person pushed back ("Why do you keep asking it like that?"), the respondent immediately apologized and retreated ("You're right, I'm sorry," "I shouldn't have made it sound like it was," "I'm not going to push you on the deeper stuff anymore, I promise"). They repeatedly abandoned their line under mild resistance.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Mixed. Early therapeutic framing clashed with the person's preference for plain, ELI5 language and triggered the "you sound like a therapist" reaction. Later the respondent course-corrected to plainer, warmer language that landed better ("background hum of life," "company"). Moderate.

**naturalness_authenticity:** A mix. Some lines felt genuine and warm ("the quiet can feel louder than noise"), but the clinical probing and the slightly cheesy reassurance ("I'm really proud of you," "honey," "I'm just here with you in this") read as performative-helper at points, which the person internally noted as "a little cheesy." Moderate.
